title: FedSecure-CL: Navigating the Privacy-Robustness Trade-off in Federated Contrastive Learning
description: This research investigates the fundamental tension between adversarial robustness and privacy in Federated Contrastive Learning (FCL). While adversarial training improves model robustness, recent evidence suggests it may increase privacy leakage. We hypothesize that FCL creates unique privacy-robustness trade-offs due to its distributed contrastive learning paradigm. We propose FedSecure-CL, a unified framework that jointly optimizes for adversarial robustness and privacy preservation through information-theoretic regularization and task-adaptive gradient perturbation.
motivation: Federated Contrastive Learning (FCL) enables collaborative learning from decentralized unlabeled data, but faces dual challenges: (1) privacy vulnerabilities from gradient leakage and membership inference attacks, and (2) a poorly understood trade-off between adversarial robustness and privacy. Recent work showed that FCL has unique membership inference vulnerabilities (Chen et al., 2024), while centralized models exhibit a privacy-robustness trade-off where adversarial training increases privacy leakage (Zhang et al., 2024). However, no work has systematically studied this trade-off in FCL or proposed unified defenses that address both threats simultaneously.
proposed_approach: We propose a three-phase research approach: (1) Systematic evaluation of privacy-robustness trade-offs in FCL across datasets (CIFAR-10/100) and attacks (gradient leakage, membership inference); (2) Mechanism analysis using information-theoretic tools to understand why FCL creates unique trade-offs; (3) FedSecure-CL, a unified defense framework combining adaptive contrastive loss with privacy regularization, task-adaptive gradient perturbation, and secure representation aggregation. The key innovation is an information-theoretic privacy loss term that penalizes membership information in representations while preserving task-relevant features.

related_work.paper: Membership Information Leakage in Federated Contrastive Learning
related_work.contribution: Demonstrated that FCL is vulnerable to membership inference attacks, establishing the privacy risks we build upon
related_work.how_ours_differs: We extend this by studying how adversarial training affects these privacy risks and propose defenses that address both privacy and robustness

related_work.paper: Learning Robust and Privacy-Preserving Representations via Information Theory
related_work.contribution: Proved inherent trade-offs between adversarial robustness and privacy in centralized settings using information theory
related_work.how_ours_differs: We study these trade-offs in the federated contrastive learning setting and propose practical defenses for this distributed scenario

related_work.paper: DeMem: Privacy-Enhanced Robust Adversarial Learning via De-Memorization
related_work.contribution: Proposed a defense against privacy-robustness trade-offs in centralized supervised learning
related_work.how_ours_differs: We address the FCL setting where contrastive learning and federated aggregation create unique challenges not present in centralized supervised learning

related_work.contribution: Introduced gradient leakage attacks showing that gradients can reconstruct training data
related_work.how_ours_differs: We study how adversarial training affects gradient leakage vulnerability in FCL and propose defenses

related_work.paper: EncoderMI: Membership Inference against Pre-trained Encoders in Contrastive Learning
related_work.contribution: First membership inference attack against contrastive encoders
related_work.how_ours_differs: We extend membership inference analysis to the federated setting and study its interaction with adversarial training

hypothesis: We hypothesize that: (1) Adversarial training in FCL exacerbates privacy leakage through membership inference attacks compared to standard FCL; (2) The privacy-robustness trade-off in FCL is more severe than in centralized supervised learning due to distributed contrastive learning dynamics; (3) A unified defense framework (FedSecure-CL) that jointly optimizes for privacy and robustness can achieve better trade-offs than naive combinations of existing techniques.

confirmation: Empirically demonstrate that adversarial training increases privacy leakage in FCL (statistically significant increase in MIA success, p < 0.05)
Show that the privacy-robustness trade-off is more pronounced in FCL than centralized learning
FedSecure-CL achieves better privacy-utility-robustness trade-offs than baselines on at least 2 of 3 datasets
success_criteria.refutation_scenario: If adversarial training does not increase privacy leakage in FCL, we will explain why FCL differs from centralized settings, identify contributing architectural features, and provide alternative insights about the relationship between security and privacy in federated learning.
success_criteria.minimal_viable_result: First systematic benchmark of privacy-robustness trade-offs in FCL, open-source implementation of privacy attacks adapted for FCL, and empirical analysis of how adversarial training affects FCL representations.

optimization_strategies: Use ResNet-18 instead of larger backbones
Reduce training epochs for proof-of-concept experiments
Focus primarily on CIFAR-10, use others for validation
Cache pretrained encoders to avoid retraining

expected_contributions: First characterization of privacy-robustness trade-offs in federated contrastive learning
FedSecure-CL: A unified framework for privacy-preserving and adversarially robust FCL
Information-theoretic analysis of why FCL creates unique privacy challenges
Open-source benchmark for evaluating privacy attacks in FCL
